title: Citizens Are Open to Public AI Services. Governments Must Seize the Opportunity.
gist: BCG's 2026 Digital Government Citizen Survey (44 countries, 48,500 respondents) finds that citizen dissatisfaction with government digital services correlates with a perception that governments are moving too slowly on AI, not too fast, though comfort levels and oversight demands vary sharply by region and by users' own AI proficiency.

themes.theme: The speed paradox in government AI adoption

insights.claim: Lower citizen satisfaction with government digital services correlates with citizens believing their government is adopting AI too slowly, not too quickly, contradicting narratives that emphasize public pushback against AI.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Governments (and vendors selling to them) can treat citizen impatience, not caution, as the dominant sentiment risk — slow-walking AI rollout may itself be depressing satisfaction scores.
insights.quote: lower citizen satisfaction levels with these services correlates with citizens believing that their governments are moving too slowly in adopting AI, rather than too quickly
insights.locator: A critical insight from the 2026 survey

insights.claim: The 2026 BCG Digital Government Citizen Survey (DGCS) concludes governments now have a public mandate to scale up AI in digital services, arriving at what the report calls an inflection point driven by the rapid expansion of AI and the rise of agentic AI.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Public-sector AI deployment is entering an acceleration phase; watch for expanded agentic-AI use cases in government services over the next survey cycle.
insights.locator: A critical insight from the 2026 survey

themes.theme: AI proficiency and the trust ceiling

insights.claim: Citizens who are more proficient with AI are seven times more optimistic about AI's benefits versus its risks than less proficient users, but the most experienced users at the very top of the proficiency curve show greater caution rather than continuing to grow more bullish.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Optimism about AI doesn't scale linearly with familiarity — expect an inverted-U pattern where power users become the source of the most pointed scrutiny, so governments should calibrate rollout pace rather than assume more exposure always means more support.
insights.quote: the individuals who are more proficient with AI are seven times more optimistic about its benefits versus its risks. At the very top of the proficiency curve, however, the most experienced users demonstrate greater caution
insights.locator: In the past two years, the proportion

insights.claim: Despite growing openness to AI, nearly two-thirds of citizens still want human oversight embedded in the structure of government digital services.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Any government or vendor AI-services rollout needs a visible human-in-the-loop layer to retain legitimacy — full automation without oversight risks a trust backlash even where openness to AI is otherwise rising.
insights.locator: A critical insight from the 2026 survey

insights.claim: The Middle East and Africa region remains the most comfortable with AI of any region globally, even after a 9-percentage-point shift toward a more neutral view in the 2026 survey.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Even the most AI-receptive region is cooling slightly — a leading indicator that global AI-sentiment tailwinds may be plateauing even in the friendliest markets.
insights.locator: Despite an overall trajectory of increasing openness

insights.claim: The Americas moved 5 percentage points toward citizens saying AI's benefits outweigh its risks, while Europe registered the lowest AI comfort of any region — 9 percentage points below the global average — and Asia-Pacific sat roughly at the global average.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/miguel-carrasco
insights.so_what: Regional AI-sentiment gaps are widening, not converging — public-sector AI strategies (and political risk around them) should be localized rather than treated as a single global trend, with Europe the laggard on public trust.
insights.locator: Despite an overall trajectory of increasing openness
